feed.title: Combined summary - Aligning privacy incentives in P2MR

entry.title: Combined summary - Aligning privacy incentives in P2MR
entry.summary: The ongoing discussions and proposals regarding Bitcoin's adaptation strategies in response to potential quantum computing threats have highlighted several key issues and viewpoints within the community. A significant focus has been placed on the modifications to BIP360, particularly concerning the P2MR (Pay-to-Migration-Request) transactions. The proposed changes aim to prohibit depth-zero script trees by requiring a merkle authentication path in the control block of P2MR transactions. This would enforce a minimum requirement for a depth-1 tree, thereby eliminating the incentive to use simpler, less private scripting methods under P2MR, aligning its efficiency more closely with that of P2TR (Pay-to-Taproot). Antoine strongly advocates for the adoption of P2TRv2 over P2MR, citing concerns about the higher transaction costs associated with P2MR, which could potentially discourage users from opting into this system despite its long-range protection against quantum threats. He emphasizes that systemic migration is crucial and should not be hindered by barriers that discourage user participation. Antoine's analysis underscores the need for cryptographic migration strategies that align closely with real-world user behaviors and economic realities to ensure both practicality and broad adoption. Recent reports from [mempool.space](https://www.mempool.space) and [Galaxy Digital](https://www.galaxydigital.com) indicate a slow adoption rate for new Bitcoin address formats, reflecting the modest impact that incentives have traditionally had on accelerating the uptake of new technologies within the Bitcoin ecosystem. Discussions also consider the broader implications of implementing post-quantum cryptography (PQC) and the potential future scenarios involving significant cryptographic events that could necessitate a rapid deployment of a pre-prepared rescue protocol. The debate extends to the efficiency of transactions and the integration of post-quantum signatures into the Bitcoin network. Proposals have been made to increase the witness discount for transactions using EC Schnorr within the P2MR framework, potentially reducing costs and encouraging wider adoption. However, Boris Nagaev expresses preference for the P2MR format due to its inherent quantum resistance, though he acknowledges the challenges associated with coordinating forks and upgrades to achieve security. In terms of protocol adjustments specifically for EC public key recovery in P2MR, a reduction of 35 bytes in the witness by eliminating the script-containing witness element has been suggested, as detailed in an online source found [here](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/public-key-recovery-for-ec-leaves-in-p2mr-bip-360/2603). These discussions emphasize the importance of preparing for potential quantum breakthroughs while maintaining trust and stability in Bitcoin, advocating for strategic approaches that integrate feature-rich cryptographic solutions without imposing undue burdens before such threats materialize. Overall, the discourse within the Bitcoin Development Mailing List reflects a community deeply engaged in balancing the immediate needs with long-term security considerations, aiming to optimize Bitcoin's response to evolving technological threats while minimizing disruptions and ensuring the currency's resilience.
